assessment: STRONGEST candidate of the search. A main-sequence star (no IR excess, W1-W2~0) fading ~15%% MONOTONICALLY and ACHROMATICALLY (g and r) over 7.4 yr with 791 epochs; not a known variable; the only fader in its 1.3-deg field, so not a per-CCD systematic. Resists the dusty-disk, single-band, slow-variable-giant, and shared-epoch explanations. A genuine slow-dimming star worthy of independent follow-up before any claim.
followups: Gaia DR3 full row: parallax/distance, RUWE (binarity), phot_variable_flag, proper motion (blend-motion test)
cross-survey confirmation: ASAS-SN / ATLAS light curve (15%% fade is detectable at V~16)

asassn.note: real fade confirmed in an independent survey; amplitude partly inflated by V->g band switch

final_verdict: REAL but MUNDANE. A single (RUWE 0.98) K-type main-sequence dwarf at 535 pc that Gaia already flags VARIABLE; the slow ~15%% fade is cross-survey-confirmed (ASAS-SN 8.8 sigma) so it is a genuine dimming star, NOT an artifact -- but the most plausible cause is a long-term magnetic activity / starspot cycle, ubiquitous in active K/M dwarfs. NOT a KIC 8462852 analogue (which is a hot, inactive F3 star). The follow-up worked: it confirmed reality and correctly identified the mundane stellar-activity explanation.
lesson: HR main-sequence cut admits active K/M dwarfs whose activity-cycle fading mimics enshrouding. Add a colour cut (BP-RP < ~0.9, F/G) to the secular-fade channel so a survivor is genuinely anomalous.
